event: The Holy Family with Saints Elizabeth and John
symbols: two infants together — the meeting of the forerunner and the Messiah, echoing the Visitation
Elizabeth bowing toward infant John — the elder kinswoman caring for the one who will prepare the way
Joseph leaning on staff — the guardian watching over both families
architectural setting — the domestic life of the two holy families in Nazareth or Judea
Mary holding Jesus toward John — prefiguring the baptism at the Jordan
description: An interior scene with classical columns. At the left, the Virgin Mary in a white head covering and orange-red robe holds the Christ Child, who leans down. At the right, an elderly woman in a blue robe (Elizabeth) bends over a nude infant (John the Baptist) who stands and reaches toward Jesus. Behind them, Joseph leans on a staff, looking down at the children. The composition is balanced and classical, rendered in warm clear colors with careful attention to anatomy and drapery.
theological_description: This composition brings together the two families whose destinies intersect at the heart of the Gospel narrative. The meeting of the two infants echoes the Visitation (LUK.1.39-45), when the unborn John leaped in Elizabeth's womb at the sound of Mary's greeting. Here the children are old enough to interact, creating a devotional scene that compresses the hidden years into a single intimate moment. Elizabeth, whom Luke describes as advanced in years (LUK.1.7), bends over her miraculous son with the same wonder she expressed at the Visitation: 'How is it that the mother of my Lord should come to me?' (LUK.1.43). The infant John reaching toward Jesus prefigures his entire prophetic ministry: 'He must increase, but I must decrease' (JHN.3.30). Joseph's quiet, watchful presence fulfills his guardian role. Poussin's classical clarity serves the theology: these are not mythological figures but real families in real domestic spaces, the ordinary setting in which the extraordinary mystery of salvation unfolds.
